## Security & Compliance

> âš ď¸Ź LOCKED â€” These rules cannot be overridden.

### Secrets & Credentials

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No hardcoded secrets
NEVER commit API keys, passwords, tokens, or private keys. Flag patterns: `sk_live_`, `AKIA`, `-----BEGIN RSA`, `password=`. Reference secrets via environment variables or Vault. Every PR with a literal secret MUST be blocked.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER use string concatenation, template literals, or f-strings to build SQL/LDAP/OS commands with user input. ALWAYS use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Example: use `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [id])` instead of `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id)`.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. For local development, use mkcert for trusted certificates. Flag any TLS bypass as CRITICAL in all HTTP clients, databases, and API calls.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256+ for stored data. Passwords MUST use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2â€”NEVER MD5/SHA. NEVER implement custom cryptography; use established libraries only. Example: bcrypt with cost factor 12 for password hashing, TLS for all API endpoints.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
NEVER skip authorization checks on authenticated endpoints. MUST verify the caller is authorized for the specific operation on the specific resource (object-level ownership checks on /:id routes, role verification on admin endpoints). Server-side enforcement is mandatory; client-side guards are insufficient. Example: validate user_id matches authenticated user before GET /users/:id.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, session management, or OAuth flows. MUST use the project's established auth system or proven provider (Auth0, Firebase, Cognito, Clerk). Custom auth introduces timing attacks and token vulnerabilities.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, rm, force-push, or reset operations without explicit confirmation. Require opt-in flags for irreversible actions. Prefer soft deletes over hard deletes. AI tools MUST NOT autonomously execute destructive operations in production.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
NEVER run ad-hoc SQL or ALTER TABLE outside migration files. MUST use Drizzle/Prisma/Alembic/Flyway for all schema changes. Every migration requires a rollback. NEVER connect to production for manual queries. Direct modifications bypass review and rollback capability.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER expose stack traces, SQL, file paths, or internal IDs in API responses. MUST return: `{"error":{"code":"SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE","message":"user-safe text","requestId":".."}}`. Route all errors through centralized error handler.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log or expose PII (names, emails, phone numbers, SSNs, IPs, tokens, credit cards). Mask sensitive fields in all logs, API responses, and error messages. Use redaction: `email: [REDACTED]` instead of actual values.

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks or bare `except: pass`. All errors MUST be handled (log with context, retry, throw, or fallback). If safe to ignore, add a comment explaining why. Silent failures cause undebugable outages.

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
MUST include tests for all new functions, endpoints, and behavioral changes. Minimum: happy path + one error case + boundary condition. Use project's test framework and place tests alongside source or in established test directory.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
MUST implement data access through the repository pattern layer. Do NOT query databases directly in controllers or servicesâ€”route all data operations through repositories.

## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all external inputs (query params, request bodies, config values) for type, range, and format before use. Example: enforce limit > 0 and < MAX, validate amount as positive number. NEVER assume inputs are safe.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
ALWAYS log structured objects, never string concatenation. Example: `logger.info({action: 'auth', user_id: 123, status: 'success'})` not `logger.info('user 123 authenticated')`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Tailwind CSS usage
MUST use Tailwind CSS for all styling. Do NOT use inline styles or custom CSS classesâ€”use Tailwind utility classes exclusively for consistency.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
MUST use consistent error response structure across all APIs: {code, message, details}. Include HTTP status codes, error codes, and user-facing messages. Example: {code: 'INVALID_EMAIL', message: 'Email format invalid', status: 400}.
